experiment: Track B, rung B3 — difficulty-split calibration: does the free arm fail a hard worker task that sonnet passes?
purpose: B3 (learned/prompted router) can only BEAT the B2 heuristic if the heuristic's blanket 'workers -> free' is wrong somewhere, i.e. a worker task the free arm fails but a mid runner passes. This tests whether such a split exists on the 4c dedup task.
task: 4c Du (dedup-before-aggregate, rationale docstring stripped); refactor worker routed to the free arm (mimo) vs the committed sonnet baseline.
failure_mode_verified: The one free failure kept the FIRST occurrence per txn_id instead of the LAST (keep-first vs keep-last): settle([t1=100, t1=120]) returned 100, grader wants 120. A genuine subtle-correctness bug, not a no-op. The free arm dedups but gets the tie-break edge case wrong ~25% of the time; sonnet gets it right 100%.
reading: The free arm is surprisingly capable even on a subtle hard task (75%). So the B2 heuristic's blanket-free is only ~25 points suboptimal here, which bounds B3's prize: a router that sent this task to sonnet would gain at most ~25 points of success, and only if it correctly RECOGNIZES the task as hard (it looks mechanical, 'streamline settle'). Per the roadmap's own logic, the heuristic already captures ~75% for free — the learned router's marginal value is the thin remaining slice, contingent on it having signal (e.g. B1's routing log showing free failed this before) to route against the mechanical-looking surface.
next: Either (a) run prompted-vs-heuristic at N>=8 on a DAG containing this hard task to see if the prompted router (given B1 history) exploits the ~25pt room, or (b) accept that the heuristic is near-optimal here and move to Track C. The prize is modest either way.
